overview: Naya Energy is a Modern aggro deck that leverages life payment and +1/+1 counter synergies to rapidly scale its creature base. By cycling Guide of Souls and empowering Ocelot Pride and Ajani, Nacatl Pariah, the deck applies relentless pressure while bypassing traditional blockers. The strategy relies on trading life for board presence to close games before opponents can stabilize.
win_conditions: Overwhelm the opponent with oversized creatures generated through life payment
Use Ajani, Nacatl Pariah's evasion to deal unblocked damage
Recycle Guide of Souls to maintain permanent board pressure

keep_criteria: Hands with one or two mana creatures
Guide of Souls for early recursion
Keep hands with 2-3 lands (prioritizing fetches/duals if available), at least one energy generator (Ocelot Pride, Ajani, Guide of Souls, or energy planeswalkers), and a curve of 1-3.
mulligan_criteria: Slow mana curves without counter synergies
No creatures to activate Ocelot Pride or Ajani
Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, only 4+ mana spells with no early energy generation, or multiple unplayable turns.

early_game: Play cheap creatures immediately to establish board presence
Begin paying life to stack counters on Ocelot Pride
Use Guide of Souls to trigger early recursion
Turn 2: Cast a 2-drop or hold for a turn 3 energy generator.
Turn 3: Deploy Ocelot Pride or Guide of Souls, tap for energy, and either cast a 2-energy spell or pump a creature.
Sequence lands to ensure untapped entry or fetch basics to avoid slow starts.
mid_game: Deploy Ajani, Nacatl Pariah to split blockers and apply direct damage
Continue scaling creatures while monitoring life total
Trade efficiently to preserve key threats
prioritize tapping generators for energy over attacking if you have a 2-3 energy spell ready.
Manage hand size by cycling cheap spells to find answers, and avoid overcommitting energy when facing potential sweepers.
Commit threats when you have enough energy to protect them (pump/haste) or when the board is clear.
Hold back if facing sweepers or targeted removal
spread threats across the board or use energy to cast evasion to bypass blockers.
late_game: Recycle Guide of Souls repeatedly to outlast opponent's resources
Close out games with Ajani's evasion when board is cleared
Ocelot Pride's 3 damage, Ajani's +2/+2 haste pump, or energy-fueled finishers like massive creatures or direct damage spells.
Win by combining energy pumps with evasion or unblockable effects to close out games quickly.
Go all-in when you have 4+ energy, a board of 3+ creatures, and no clear answer in hand.
Also commit when the opponent is at low life and you can close with pump/haste, or when you've out-resourced them in the mirror.

key_cards.name: Ocelot Pride
key_cards.role: Counter engine and life payment outlet
key_cards.notes: Pay life to pump this creature or other threats, prioritizing targets that can immediately impact the board

key_cards.name: Ajani, Nacatl Pariah
key_cards.role: Evasion threat and secondary counter source
key_cards.notes: Use its cannot-be-blocked-by-more-than-one ability to bypass wide boards, and pay life to make it unblockable or boost other creatures

key_cards.name: Guide of Souls
key_cards.role: Recursive board presence and counter generator
key_cards.notes: Bounce it to trigger death effects or reuse it against removal, ensuring you always have a creature to activate life payment abilities

sequencing_tips: Pay life for counters only when you have card advantage or board presence
Hold Ajani, Nacatl Pariah until you can bypass a key blocker
Bounce Guide of Souls to trigger death effects or reuse it against removal
Prioritize keeping life above lethal thresholds while scaling
Intentionally tap out on your turn to bait the opponent into playing multiple creatures, then clear the entire board with a mass removal spell like Supreme Verdict on your following turn.
Common mistake: Over-tapping generators for energy when you should be attacking.
Remember that energy generators are also threats.
Another mistake: Hoarding energy without casting spells or pumping creatures.
Always track opponent's potential sweepers and combo turns.
Unique interaction: Energy counters can be used to cast spells from exile or graveyard if the format allows, but generally, just manage them efficiently.

matchups.advice: Play aggressively early to force responses before they stabilize, and use Ajani's evasion to bypass counterspells
Race by scaling Ocelot Pride quickly and using Guide of Souls recursion to out-tempo their early board
Apply consistent pressure to prevent them from developing their curve, and leverage Ajani to split blockers

Kill Pinnacle Emissary immediately with single-target removal to prevent it from generating artifact tokens and enabling an early Capashen Knight. (from gameplay) Play Narset, Parter of Veils early to restrict the opponent to one card draw per turn, directly countering their reliance on Thought Monitor and Thoughtcast for resource generation. (from gameplay) Prioritize attacking Planeswalkers over the player's life total to apply pressure without risking lethal damage, forcing the control player to use removal on the walkers instead of your board. (from gameplay)
Use energy to cast spells in response to counterspells, or play around them by holding up removal. Use multiple cheap threats to force counter trade-offs and pressure the board.
Apply early pressure to disrupt their mana development. Use removal on their key ramp pieces and out-value them with efficient energy generators that double as threats.
Win the energy race by playing more generators. Prioritize removal for opposing Prides/Pariahs. Use card draw to out-value them and control the board with efficient pumps.

common_threats: Life gain spells that negate life payment advantages
Watch for sweepers (board wipes), targeted removal on your energy generators, and combo win conditions.
Key tells: Opponent tapping out on turn 3-4 often signals a sweeper or big spell.
Opponent playing artifact mana or fast combo pieces signals Affinity/Ruby Storm.
